era_context: The Thirty Years' War is in its eighth year, with religious conflict in Central Europe intensifying. The Mughal Empire under Jahangir is in its final years, with power struggles emerging over succession. The Ming Dynasty in China is experiencing increasing internal strain and external pressure from the Jurchen. European colonial expansion accelerates, with the Dutch and English challenging Iberian dominance in trade and territorial control.

events.title: Battle of Lützen; Gustavus Adolphus defeats Wallenstein
events.description: The Battle of Lützen on 6 November 1626 was a major engagement of the Thirty Years' War in which Swedish King Gustavus Adolphus defeated the Imperial commander Count Wallenstein. The battle demonstrated the effectiveness of Swedish military tactics and the superiority of Gustavus Adolphus's army. However, the battle was costly and indecisive in strategic terms; neither side achieved a decisive victory. The engagement marked a turning point in the Thirty Years' War, as Swedish intervention shifted the balance of power away from the Holy Roman Empire. Gustavus Adolphus's military innovations, including the use of mobile artillery and linear formations, influenced European warfare for generations. The battle is often cited as a precursor to the Swedish king's greater victories in 1630–1632.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the Thirty Years' War (Parker, G., The Thirty Years' War, 2nd ed., 1997)

events.certainty_note: The battle date of 6 November 1626 is precisely documented in contemporary sources. The engagement and its significance are confirmed.

events.title: Peter Minuit purchases Manhattan from Lenape; New Amsterdam founded
events.region: Manhattan, New Amsterdam (New York), North America
events.description: On 24 May 1626, Peter Minuit, director of the Dutch West India Company's colony in North America, purchased Manhattan Island from the Lenape people for goods valued at approximately 60 guilders (often cited as 'beads and trinkets' in popular accounts, though the actual goods are debated). The purchase established New Amsterdam as the capital of the Dutch colony of New Netherland. The transaction is often portrayed as a fraudulent bargain, but modern scholarship suggests the Lenape may have understood it as a temporary use agreement rather than permanent alienation of land. New Amsterdam grew into a major trading post and eventually became New York City after English conquest in 1664. The purchase is a symbolic moment in European colonisation of North America, though it obscures the complex negotiations and misunderstandings between European and indigenous peoples.
key_figures: Lenape leaders (names not recorded in European sources)

sources.name: Dutch West India Company records and correspondence
Historiographic consensus on New Amsterdam (Shorto, R., The Island at the Center of the World, 2004)

events.certainty_note: The purchase date of 24 May 1626 is documented in Dutch records. The transaction occurred, though its interpretation is debated.

events.title: Jahangir's health declines; succession struggles intensify in Mughal court
events.description: In 1626, Mughal Emperor Jahangir's health deteriorated significantly, prompting succession struggles within the imperial court. Jahangir's wife Nur Jahan and various princes competed for influence and position. Shah Jahan, Jahangir's son, was positioned as the likely successor but faced rivals. The succession crisis reflected the Mughal Empire's vulnerability to internal power struggles despite its apparent strength. Jahangir died in 1627, and Shah Jahan succeeded him, consolidating power and initiating a new phase of Mughal expansion and cultural patronage. The succession struggle of 1626–1627 demonstrated the importance of court factions and the role of women (particularly Nur Jahan) in Mughal politics.

sources.name: Mughal court records (Badshah Nama, compiled later)
European traveller accounts (Jean-Baptiste Tavernier, Francois Bernier)
Historiographic consensus on Mughal succession (Richards, J.F., The Mughal Empire, 1993)

events.certainty_note: Jahangir's declining health in 1626 is documented in Mughal records and European accounts. The succession struggles are confirmed, though details are sometimes unclear.

events.title: English Parliament dissolved by King Charles I; Personal Rule begins
events.description: King Charles I dissolved Parliament on 15 March 1626 after a contentious session in which Parliament refused to grant adequate taxation and criticised his favourite, the Duke of Buckingham. The dissolution marked the beginning of Charles I's 'Personal Rule' (1626–1640), during which he governed without calling Parliament. Charles I attempted to raise revenue through controversial methods, including the revival of feudal dues and the extension of ship money to inland counties. The Personal Rule deepened the constitutional crisis and alienated Parliament, the nobility, and common people. The period is often seen as a prelude to the English Civil War (1642–1651). The dissolution of 1626 was a critical moment in English constitutional history, as it demonstrated the king's willingness to govern without parliamentary consent.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on early Stuart England (Russell, C., The Fall of the British Monarchies, 1991)

events.certainty_note: The dissolution date of 15 March 1626 is precisely documented in parliamentary and royal records. The event and its significance are uncontested.

events.title: Ming Dynasty loses control of Liaodong; Later Jin expands territory
events.description: In 1626, the Ming Dynasty lost effective control of Liaodong to the Later Jin state under Hong Taiji. The Jurchen conquest of Liaodong was a major strategic victory that gave the Later Jin control of valuable territory and resources. The loss of Liaodong marked a critical turning point in the Ming-Jurchen conflict, as the Later Jin now had a secure base for further expansion. The Ming Dynasty's inability to recover Liaodong demonstrated its declining military capacity and the rise of Jurchen power. The conquest of Liaodong was a precursor to the Later Jin's eventual conquest of all of China and the establishment of the Qing Dynasty in 1644. The event is often cited as a key moment in the Ming Dynasty's decline.

sources.name: Ming court records and official histories (Ming Shilu)
Historiographic consensus on Ming decline (Huang, R., 1587: A Year of No Significance, 1981)

events.certainty_note: The loss of Liaodong in 1626 is documented in Ming records. The strategic significance is confirmed by modern scholarship.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of Lützen is sometimes portrayed as a clear Swedish victory, but modern scholarship emphasises its indecisiveness and the heavy casualties on both sides. The battle's strategic impact is often overstated; it did not immediately shift the balance of the Thirty Years' War decisively in Sweden's favour. The Manhattan purchase is often cited as a fraudulent bargain, but modern historians debate whether the Lenape understood it as a permanent land sale or a temporary use agreement; the characterisation of the transaction as obviously fraudulent may reflect modern assumptions rather than historical reality. Jahangir's succession crisis is sometimes portrayed as more dramatic than sources suggest; the transition to Shah Jahan appears to have been relatively smooth despite court factionalism.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1626 is rich for European political and military events, reflecting the abundance of English, Dutch, Swedish, and Imperial archival sources. Mughal sources are substantial but often compiled decades after events. Chinese sources on the Ming-Jurchen conflict are detailed but filtered through official histories. The Manhattan purchase is documented from the Dutch perspective but entirely lacks indigenous Lenape documentation or perspective. The year is well-documented for elite political and military events but poorly documented for economic conditions, demographic changes, or the experiences of common people, enslaved populations, or indigenous societies.

graph_edges.note: The Battle of Lützen of 1626 was a precursor to Gustavus Adolphus's greater intervention in the Thirty Years' War in 1630.
The founding of New Amsterdam in 1626 led to the establishment of a Dutch colony that was eventually conquered by England in 1664.
Jahangir's declining health in 1626 led to Shah Jahan's consolidation of power in 1627.
The dissolution of Parliament in 1626 led to the constitutional crisis that produced the Petition of Right in 1628.
The loss of Liaodong in 1626 was a critical step in the Later Jin's rise and the eventual fall of the Ming Dynasty in 1644.
